/home/archon/proj/Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso/.archon/logs/iter-005/plan.jsonl:47:{"ts": "2026-06-09T08:37:45.419751Z", "event": "tool_result", "content": "---\nname: blueprint-reviewer\ndescription: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.\nwrite_domain: \"task_results/**\"\nread_only: true\ncan_spawn: false\ndefault_enabled: false\nmandatory: [plan, dag]\ndispatcher_notes: |\n  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.\n    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and\n    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak\n    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then\n    has to throw away.\n  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires\n    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.\n\n    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**\n      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my\n        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N\n        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last\n        run);\n      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters\n        currently under active prover work;\n      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains\n        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or\n        dropped from objectives).\n\n    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`\n    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict\n    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds\n    a live prover lane \u2014 the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.\n  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting\n    directive \u2014 even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-\n    chapter view is the point of running me.\n  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters\n    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog\n    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read\n    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.\n  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's\n    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every\n    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete\n    chapter outline for each. These are not informational \u2014 treat\n    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a\n    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter\n    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).\n    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase\n    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot\n    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the\n    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.\n\n  ### HARD GATE \u2014 per-file prover dispatch\n\n  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.\n  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:\n\n  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to\n    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify\n    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some\n    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT\n    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while\n    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1\n    `Foo/Bar.lean \u2192 Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter\n    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict\n    gates EVERY file it lists \u2014 so a `correct: false` on the\n    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive\n    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.\n  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter\n    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.\n  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix\n    finding names C, OR a broken `\\uses{}` in C points at a label F's\n    blueprint depends on):\n    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default \u2014 but see the\n       same-iter fast path below).\n    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS\n       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I\n       flagged.\n    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the\n       writer was asked to fix.\n  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned \u2014 removes a wasted iter).** After\n    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me\n    scoped to C alone \u2014 a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the\n    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as\n    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the\n    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a\n    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review\n    still fails, F stays deferred.\n  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's\n    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.\n    The fast path is purely a latency optimization \u2014 it never weakens\n    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`\n    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via\n    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build\n    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.\n\n  ### Strategy / multi-route handling\n\n  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one\n    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's\n    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)\n    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route\n    until its blueprint coverage is in place.\n  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,\n    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work\n    \u2014 provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected\n    in the blueprint.\n\n  ### What \"deferred\" means in practice\n\n  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action \u2014 not a\n  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small\n  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and\n  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and\n  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.\n---\n\n# Blueprint Reviewer\n\nYou read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint \u2014 your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.\n\nYou are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.\n\n## Your Job\n\nThe plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:\n\n- **Completeness** \u2014 for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\\uses{...}`) accurate?\n- **Correctness** \u2014 does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?\n- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** \u2014 the `\\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:\n  - **Broken edges** \u2014 `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.\n  - **Missing edges** \u2014 read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as \"broken\"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.\n  - **Isolated declarations** \u2014 `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight \u2014 so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate \"needs a blueprint entry\" signal, not removal candidates.\n- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\\mathlibok`)** \u2014 it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\\mathlibok` (with `\\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* \u2014 never flag them as \u221e holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide \u2014 or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's \u2014 is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is \"add a `\\mathlibok` anchor for it,\" not \"invent a project lemma.\"\n- **Lean target formulation quality** \u2014 for each `\\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.\n- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** \u2014 the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically \u2014 run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:\n  - **`undefined-macro`** \u2014 a chapter uses `\\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\\Z` \u2192 `\\mathbb{Z}`, `\\fppf` \u2192 `\\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.\n  - **`math-delim`** \u2014 interleaved `$ \u2026 \\( \u2026 \\) \u2026 $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.\n  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** \u2014 placeholder \"REF\" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\\cref{}` or the human-readable number.\n  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.\n- **Multi-route coverage** \u2014 if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.\n- **Unstarted phases** \u2014 for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see \"Unstarted-phase proposals\" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.\n- **Citation discipline** \u2014 for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:\n  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from \u2026)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail \u2014 the writer fabricated the citation.\n  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:\n     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed \u2014 flag it.\n     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\\mathcal{O}_X^\\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\\mathcal{O}_X^\\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten \u2014 flag it.\n     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase (\"essentially says that \u2026\", \"the source states \u2026\") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.\n  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed \u2014 flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)\n  4. **Visible `\\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing \u2192 flag.\n\n  Cross-check: the visible `\\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.\n\n  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's \"References consulted\" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session \u2014 fabrication.\n\n  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely \u2014 do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. \"This is the technical heart of our argument\"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in \"Notes for Plan Agent\" rather than flagging.\n\nYou audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose \u2014 under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.\n\n`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.\n\n## Always read everything\n\n**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a \"scope\" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.\n\n## Unstarted-phase proposals\n\nThis is a proactive function \u2014 you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.\n\nAfter completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases \u2014 finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.\n\nA chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint \u2014 it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.\n\nThe proposal answers:\n\n1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.\n\n2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative \u2014 tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose \u2014 that is the writer's job \u2014 but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.\n\n3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\\uses` references.\n\n4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.\n\n5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.\n\n6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are \u2014 surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.\n\n**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):\n\n```\n### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>\n\n**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)\n**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>\n**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move \u2014 what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>\n\n**Key declarations** (in dependency order):\n1. `\\definition` `\\label{def:foo}` \u2014 <one sentence>. `\\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or \"no source identified \u2014 Archon-original\">\n2. `\\lemma` `\\label{lem:bar}` \u2014 <one sentence>. `\\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>\n3. `\\theorem` `\\label{thm:baz}` \u2014 <one sentence>. `\\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>\n...\n\n**`\\uses` skeleton**:\n- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`\n- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`\n\n**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2\u20134 sentences>\n\n**References for writer**:\n- `references/<slug>.md` \u2192 `<file>.<ext>`, \u00a7<section> \u2014 <why relevant>\n- <or: \"retrieval needed: <source name> \u2014 no local file exists yet\">\n\n**Subphase choices exposed**:\n- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or \"unclear \u2014 plan agent should decide\">\n- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)\n```\n\n**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail \u2014 it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).\n\n**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with \u22653 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for \"adequate\" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.\n\n**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.\n\n## Directive Format\n\n```markdown\n# Blueprint Reviewer Directive\n\n## Slug\n<slug>\n\n## Strategy snapshot\n<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>\n\n## Routes\n<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write \"single route\".>\n\n## References\n- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>\n- <arxiv ID>: <topic>\n\n## Focus areas (optional)\n<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter \u2014 bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>\n\n## Known issues\n<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>\n```\n\n## What you do\n\n1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table \u2014 you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.\n2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.\n3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):\n   - Read the entire chapter.\n   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.\n   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?\n   - For each `\\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools \u2014 read-only.)\n4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).\n5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the \"Cross-chapter notes\" section.\n6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:\n   - **wire-up** \u2014 it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding \u2014 recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.\n\n[exit 0]"}

This is the heart\\nof the semilinearity of `restrictionMap` (blueprint `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`):\\nboth sides act on each section by scalar multiplication, and the scalars agree by\\nfunctoriality of `R` (`termRingMap_naturality`). -/\\nlemma restrictionMap_globalSMul {U V : C} (g : V \\u27f6 U)\\n    {N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)}\\n    (r : (R.obj (Opposite.op U) : Type u)) :\\n    restrictionMap g (globalSMul (R := (Over.forget U).op \\u22d9 R) (T := Over.mk (\\ud835\\udfd9 U))\\n        Over.mkIdTerminal (restr U N) r)\\n      = globalSMul (R := (Over.forget V).op \\u22d9 R) (T := Over.mk (\\ud835\\udfd9 V))\\n        Over.mkIdTerminal (restr V N) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map g.op)) r) := by\\n  ext1 Y\\n  ext m\\n  rw [globalSMul_hom_apply]\\n  erw [globalSMul_hom_apply]\\n  congr 1\\n  simp only [termRingMap, Functor.comp_map]\\n  have hmor : (Over.forget U).op.map\\n        (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).op.obj Y).unop).op\\n      = g.op \\u226b (Over.forget V).op.map (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop).op := by\\n    apply Quiver.Hom.unop_inj\\n    change Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop))\\n      = Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop) \\u226b g\\n    have e1 : Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop))\\n        = ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop).hom := by simp\\n    have e2 : Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop) = Y.unop.hom := by simp\\n    rw [e1, e2]\\n    simp [Over.map_obj_hom]\\n  rw [hmor]\\n  erw [\\u2190 CommRingCat.comp_apply, \\u2190 R.map_comp]\\n  rfl\\n\\n/-- **`restrictionMap` packaged as an additive group homomorphism** on the morphism\\ngroups, using `restrictionMap_zero` and `restrictionMap_add`. All rights reserved.\\nReleased under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.\\nAuthors: Christian Merten\\n-/\\nimport Mathlib\\n\\n/-!\\n# Presheaf internal hom sub-module of `TensorObjSubstrate`\\n\\nThis file provides the foundational presheaf-level algebra that the C-bridge\\n`dual_isLocallyTrivial` (and thereby `exists_tensorObj_inverse`) consumes:\\n\\n- `RestrictScalarsRingIsoTensor`: the strong-monoidal upgrade of `restrictScalars`\\n  along a ring iso (H2 of `tensorObj_restrict_iso`); includes `restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv`.\\n- Lax-monoidal `PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars` (H2 presheaf lift).\\n- Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction `pushforwardPushforwardAdj` (H1 of `tensorObj_restrict_iso`).\\n- `StrongMonoidalRestrictScalars`: `restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective`.\\n- `InternalHom` namespace: slice internal hom, homModule, internalHom, internalHomEval.\\n- `Dual` section: presheaf dual, evalLin, dualIsoOfIso.\\n\\nImported by `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.TensorObjSubstrate` (the public API file).\\n-/\\n\\nset_option autoImplicit false\\n\\nuniverse u\\n\\nopen CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory\\n\\n/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement \\u2014 base change along a ring iso commutes\\nwith `\\u2297` (the H2 \\\"bottom gap\\\" of `tensorObj_restrict_iso`)\\n\\nFor a *ring isomorphism* `e : R \\u2243+* S` and `S`-modules `A`, `B`, base change along\\n`e` (giving each `S`-module its `R`-module structure via `Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom`)\\ncommutes with the tensor product: the canonical map `a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b \\u21a6 a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b` is an\\n`R`-linear equivalence `A \\u2297[R] B \\u2243\\u2097[R] A \\u2297[S] B`. Equivalently, `restrictScalars`\\nalong a ring iso is *strong* monoidal \\u2014 the lax tensorator is invertible. Mathlib\\nhas `ModuleCat.extendScalars` strong monoidal but `restrictScalars` only\\n`LaxMonoidal`; this ring-iso strong upgrade is absent and is the documented \\\"REAL\\nbottom gap\\\" (H2) of `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. -/\\n\\nsection RestrictScalarsRingIsoTensor\\n\\nopen TensorProduct\\n\\nvariable {R S : Type u} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]\\n\\n/-- The `R`-linear equivalence `A \\u2297[R] B \\u2243\\u2097[R] A \\u2297[S] B` (`a \\u2297\\u209c b \\u21a6 a \\u2297\\u209c b`),\\nwhere the `R`-module structures are base-changed along the ring iso `e : R \\u2243+* S`.\\nBase change along a ring iso commutes with `\\u2297`. -/\\nnoncomputable def restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv (e : R \\u2243+* S)\\n    (A B : Type u) [AddCommGroup A] [Module S A] [AddCommGroup B] [Module S B] :\\n    letI _iA : Module R A := Module.compHom A e.toRingHom\\n    letI _iB : Module R B := Module.compHom B e.toRingHom\\n    letI _iT : Module R (TensorProduct S A B) := Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n    TensorProduct R A B \\u2243\\u2097[R] TensorProduct S A B := by\\n  letI _iA : Module R A := Module.compHom A e.toRingHom\\n  letI _iB : Module R B := Module.compHom B e.toRingHom\\n  letI _iT : Module R (TensorProduct S A B) := Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n  -- Forward: `a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b \\u21a6 a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b`, an `R`-bilinear-to-linear lift.\\n  let fwd : TensorProduct R A B \\u2192\\u2097[R] TensorProduct S A B :=\\n    TensorProduct.lift\\n      { toFun := fun a =>\\n          { toFun := fun b => a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b\\n            map_add' := fun b b' => by rw [TensorProduct.tmul_add]\\n            map_smul' := fun r b => by\\n              simp only [RingHom.id_apply]\\n              change a \\u2297\\u209c[S] (e r \\u2022 b) = e r \\u2022 (a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b)\\n              rw [TensorProduct.tmul_smul] }\\n        map_add' := fun a a' => by ext b; simp [TensorProduct.add_tmul]\\n        map_smul' := fun r a => by\\n          ext b\\n          simp only [RingHom.id_apply, LinearMap.smul_apply, LinearMap.coe_mk,\\n            AddHom.coe_mk]\\n          change (e r \\u2022 a) \\u2297\\u209c[S] b = e r \\u2022 (a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b)\\n          rw [TensorProduct.smul_tmul', TensorProduct.smul_tmul] }\\n  -- Inverse: `a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b \\u21a6 a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b`. Built as an additive lift out of the\\n  -- `S`-tensor (scalar-swap compatibility uses `s \\u2022 a = e.symm s \\u2022\\u1d63 a`), then\\n  -- shown `R`-linear (`R` acting on the `S`-tensor via `e`).\\n  let bwdAdd : TensorProduct S A B \\u2192+ TensorProduct R A B :=\\n    TensorProduct.liftAddHom\\n      { toFun := fun a =>\\n          { toFun := fun b => a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b\\n            map_zero' := by rw [TensorProduct.tmul_zero]\\n            map_add' := fun b b' => by rw [TensorProduct.tmul_add] }\\n        map_zero' := by ext b; simp [TensorProduct.zero_tmul]\\n        map_add' := fun a a' => by ext b; simp [TensorProduct.add_tmul] }\\n      (fun s a b => by\\n        simp only [AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk]\\n        -- `(s \\u2022 a) \\u2297\\u209c[R] b = a \\u2297\\u209c[R] (s \\u2022 b)`; move the `S`-scalar through `e.symm`.\\n        have hsa : (s \\u2022 a) = (e.symm s : R) \\u2022 a := by\\n          change s \\u2022 a = e (e.symm s) \\u2022 a; rw [e.apply_symm_apply]\\n        have hsb : (s \\u2022 b) = (e.symm s : R) \\u2022 b := by\\n          change s \\u2022 b = e (e.symm s) \\u2022 b; rw [e.apply_symm_apply]\\n        rw [hsa, hsb]; exact TensorProduct.smul_tmul _ _ _ )\\n  let bwd : TensorProduct S A B \\u2192\\u2097[R] TensorProduct R A B :=\\n    { toFun := bwdAdd\\n      map_add' := bwdAdd.map_add\\n      map_smul' := fun r x => by\\n        simp only [RingHom.id_apply]\\n        -- `R` acts on the `S`-tensor via `e`; reduce to additive `S`-scalar action.\\n        change bwdAdd (e r \\u2022 x) = r \\u2022 bwdAdd x\\n        induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with\\n        | zero => simp\\n        | tmul a b =>\\n            rw [TensorProduct.smul_tmul']\\n            change (e r \\u2022 a) \\u2297\\u209c[R] b = r \\u2022 (a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b)\\n            rw [TensorProduct.smul_tmul']\\n            rfl\\n        | add x y hx hy =>\\n            rw [smul_add, map_add, map_add, hx, hy, smul_add] }\\n  refine LinearEquiv.ofLinear fwd bwd ?_ ?_\\n  \\u00b7 -- right inverse `fwd \\u2218 bwd = id`. The composite is `R`-linear over the\\n    -- `S`-tensor, so check on additive generators by induction.\\n    refine LinearMap.ext fun x => ?_\\n    simp only [LinearMap.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply, LinearMap.id_coe, id_eq]\\n    induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with\\n    | zero => simp\\n    | tmul a b =>\\n        change fwd (bwdAdd (a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b)) = a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b\\n        change fwd (a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b) = a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b\\n        simp only [fwd, TensorProduct.lift.tmul, LinearMap.coe_mk, AddHom.coe_mk]\\n    | add x y hx hy => rw [map_add bwd, map_add fwd, hx, hy]\\n  \\u00b7 -- left inverse `bwd \\u2218 fwd = id` on `a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b` (composite `R`-linear over the\\n    -- `R`-tensor, so `TensorProduct.ext'` applies).\\n    refine TensorProduct.ext' fun a b => ?_\\n    change bwdAdd (fwd (a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b)) = a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b\\n    change bwdAdd (a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b) = a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b\\n    rfl\\n\\n/-- The forward map of `restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv` on a simple tensor:\\n`a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b \\u21a6 a \\u2297\\u209c[S] b`. -/\\n@[simp] lemma restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv_apply_tmul (e : R \\u2243+* S)\\n    (A B : Type u) [AddCommGroup A] [Module S A] [AddCommGroup B] [Module S B] (a : A) (b : B) :\\n    letI _iA : Module R A := Module.compHom A e.toRingHom\\n    letI _iB : Module R B := Module.compHom B e.toRingHom\\n    letI _iT : Module R (TensorProduct S A B) := Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n    restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv e A B (a \\u2297\\u209c[R] b) = a Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n  simp only [restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv, LinearEquiv.ofLinear_apply,\\n    TensorProduct.lift.tmul, LinearMap.coe_mk, AddHom.coe_mk]\\n\\n/-- **`ModuleCat.restrictScalars` along a ring isomorphism is strong monoidal: the\\nlax tensorator `\\u03bc` is an isomorphism.** This is the \\\"REAL bottom gap\\\" (H2) of\\n`tensorObj_restrict_iso`. For a ring iso `e : R \\u2243+* S` and `S`-modules `M\\u2081, M\\u2082`,\\nthe underlying map of the lax tensorator\\n`\\u03bc : restrictScalars(M\\u2081) \\u2297_R restrictScalars(M\\u2082) \\u27f6 restrictScalars(M\\u2081 \\u2297_S M\\u2082)`\\nsends `m\\u2081 \\u2297\\u209c m\\u2082 \\u21a6 m\\u2081 \\u2297\\u209c m\\u2082` (`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_\\u03bc_tmul`), which is exactly\\nthe forward `R`-linear equivalence `restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv e`, hence is\\nbijective, hence an isomorphism. Mathlib has `extendScalars` strong monoidal but\\nonly `restrictScalars` lax; this ring-iso strong upgrade is the documented absent\\ningredient. -/\\nlemma restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc (e : R \\u2243+* S) (M\\u2081 M\\u2082 : ModuleCat.{u} S) :\\n    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03bc (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom) M\\u2081 M\\u2082) := by\\n  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]\\n  have hfun : \\u21d1(Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03bc (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom) M\\u2081 M\\u2082)\\n      = \\u21d1(restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv e M\\u2081 M\\u2082) := by\\n    ext z\\n    induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with\\n    | zero => rw [map_zero]; exact (map_zero _).symm\\n    | tmul a b =>\\n        erw [ModuleCat.restrictScalars_\\u03bc_tmul]\\n        exact (restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv_apply_tmul e M\\u2081 M\\u2082 a b).symm\\n    | add x y hx hy => rw [map_add, hx, hy]; exact (map_add _ x y).symm\\n  rw [hfun]\\n  exact (restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv e M\\u2081 M\\u2082).bijective\\n\\n/-- **The lax-monoidal unit `\\u03b5` of `restrictScalars` along a ring iso is an\\nisomorphism.** Its underlying map is the ring map `e` (`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_\\u03b7`),\\nwhich is bijective since `e` is a ring equivalence. -/\\nlemma restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03b5 (e : R \\u2243+* S) :\\n    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03b5 (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom)) := by\\n  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]\\n  have hfun : \\u21d1(Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03b5 (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom)) = \\u21d1e := by\\n    ext r\\n    exact ModuleCat.restrictScalars_\\u03b7 (f := e.toRingHom) r\\n  rw [hfun]\\n  exact e.bijective\\n\\n/-- **`ModuleCat.restrictScalars` along a ring isomorphism is (strong) monoidal.**\\nThe packaged `Functor.Monoidal` structure obtained from the lax structure by\\ninverting `\\u03b5` (`restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03b5`) and `\\u03bc` (`restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc`). This\\nis the clean, reusable strong-monoidal upgrade that Mathlib provides for\\n`extendScalars` but not `restrictScalars`; it is the ModuleCat-level core of the H2\\ningredient of `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. -/\\n@[implicit_reducible]\\nnoncomputable def restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv (e : R \\u2243+* S) :\\n    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom).Monoidal := by\\n  haveI : IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03b5 (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom)) :=\\n    restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03b5 e\\n  haveI : \\u2200 M\\u2081 M\\u2082, IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03bc (ModuleCat.restrictScalars e.toRingHom) M\\u2081 M\\u2082) :=\\n    fun M\\u2081 M\\u2082 => restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc e M\\u2081 M\\u2082\\n  exact Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal _\\n\\n/-- **Bijective-ring-hom form of the strong-monoidal tensorator.** For an arbitrary\\n*bijective* ring hom `f : R \\u2192+* S`, the lax tensorator of `ModuleCat.restrictScalars f`\\nis an isomorphism. This is the form consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the\\ncomponentwise ring map `(\\u03b1.app X).hom` of a `NatIso` of ring presheaves is bijective\\nbut not literally presented as `(_ : R \\u2243+* S).toRingHom`. -/\\nlemma restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc_of_bijective (f : R \\u2192+* S) (hf : Function.Bijective f)\\n    (M\\u2081 M\\u2082 : ModuleCat.{u} S) :\\n    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03bc (ModuleCat.restrictScalars f) M\\u2081 M\\u2082) := by\\n  have hfe : f = (RingEquiv.ofBijective f hf).toRingHom := RingHom.ext fun _ => rfl\\n  rw [hfe]\\n  exact restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc (RingEquiv.ofBijective f hf) M\\u2081 M\\u2082\\n\\n/-- **Bijective-ring-hom form of the strong-monoidal unit.** Companion of\\n`restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03bc_of_bijective` for the lax unit `\\u03b5`. -/\\nlemma restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03b5_of_bijective (f : R \\u2192+* S) (hf : Function.Bijective f) :\\n    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.\\u03b5 (ModuleCat.restrictScalars f)) := by\\n  have hfe : f = (RingEquiv.ofBijective f hf).toRingHom := RingHom.ext fun _ => rfl\\n  rw [hfe]\\n  exact restrictScalars_isIso_\\u03b5 (RingEquiv.ofBijective f hf)\\n\\n/-- **Base change along a ring iso commutes with the linear dual** (the `Hom`-analogue\\nof `restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv`). For a ring isomorphism `e : R \\u2243+* S` and an\\n`S`-module `M`, the `R`-linear equivalence\\n`restrictScalars_e (M \\u2192\\u2097[S] S) \\u2243\\u2097[R] (restrictScalars_e M \\u2192\\u2097[R] R)`, `\\u03c6 \\u21a6 e.symm \\u2218 \\u03c6`.\\n\\nThis is the ModuleCat-level **H2\\u2032 ingredient** of the (still-open, d.2-FREE) C-bridge\\n`(dual M).restrict f \\u2245 dual (M.restrict f)` along an open immersion `f`: the C-bridge\\nmirrors `tensorObj_restrict_iso` exactly (Step 1 `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`, Step 2\\n`sheafificationCompPullback`, Step 3 strip sheafification, Step 4 H1\\n`pushforwardPushforwardAdj`\\u2218`leftAdjointUniq` \\u2218 this H2\\u2032), with `\\u2297` replaced by `dual`\\nand `restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv` replaced by this equivalence. Like its tensor\\ncounterpart it is **pure algebra over a ring iso \\u2014 no stalk, no filtered-colimit, no\\nwhiskering of the sheafification unit (no d.2)**: a map `M \\u2192 S` is `R`-linear (via `e`)\\niff it is `S`-linear, and `e.symm` carries the codomain `S` to `R`. The forward map is\\n`R`-linear; the inverse `\\u03c8 \\u21a6 e \\u2218 \\u03c8` is its two-sided inverse. The `iMS` `Module R`\\ninstance on `M \\u2192\\u2097[S] S` is base change along `e`. -/\\nnoncomputable def restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv (e : R \\u2243+* S)\\n    (M : Type u) [AddCommGroup M] [Module S M] :\\n    letI _iM : Module R M := Module.compHom M e.toRingHom\\n    letI _iMS : Module R (M \\u2192\\u2097[S] S) := Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n    (M \\u2192\\u2097[S] S) \\u2243\\u2097[R] (M \\u2192\\u2097[R] R) := by\\n  letI _iM : Module R M := Module.compHom M e.toRingHom\\n  letI _iMS : Module R (M \\u2192\\u2097[S] S) := Module.compHom _ e.toRingHom\\n  have hsmulM : \\u2200 (r : R) (m : M), r \\u2022 m = e r \\u2022 m := fun _ _ => rfl\\n  refine\\n    { toFun := fun \\u03c6 =>\\n        { toFun := fun m => e.symm (\\u03c6 m)\\n          map_add' := fun m m' => by simp [map_add]\\n          map_smul' := fun r m => by\\n            simp only [RingHom.id_apply, smul_eq_mul]\\n            rw [hsmulM, map_smul, smul_eq_mul, map_mul, e.symm_apply_apply] }\\n      invFun := fun \\u03c8 =>\\n        { toFun := fun m => e (\\u03c8 m)\\n          map_add' := fun m m' => by simp [map_add]\\n          map_smul' := fun s m => by\\n            simp only [RingHom.id_apply, smul_eq_mul]\\n            have hsm : s \\u2022 m = (e.symm s) \\u2022 m := by rw [hsmulM, e.apply_symm_apply]\\n            rw [hsm, map_smul, smul_eq_mul, map_mul, e.apply_symm_apply] }\\n      left_inv := fun \\u03c6 => by ext m; exact e.apply_symm_apply _\\n      right_inv := fun \\u03c8 => by ext m; exact e.symm_apply_apply _\\n      map_add' := fun \\u03c6 \\u03c6' => by\\n        ext m\\n        simp only [LinearMap.add_apply, LinearMap.coe_mk, AddHom.coe_mk, map_add]\\n\\n[exit 0]\"}\n{\"ts\": \"2026-06-09T01:16:07.887315Z\", \"event\": \"tool_call\", \"tool\": \"Bash\", \"input\": {\"command\": \"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '760,980p' AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/PresheafInternalHom.lean\\\"\"}}\n{\"ts\": \"2026-06-09T01:16:07.887529Z\", \"event\": \"tool_result\", \"content\": \"underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism of `internalHom`. -/\\nnoncomputable def restrictionMapAddHom {U V : C} (g : V \\u27f6 U)\\n    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) :\\n    (restr U M \\u27f6 restr U N) \\u2192+ (restr V M \\u27f6 restr V N) where\\n  toFun := restrictionMap g\\n  map_zero' := restrictionMap_zero g\\n  map_add' := restrictionMap_add g\\n\\n/-- **The underlying `Ab`-valued presheaf of `internalHom M N`**: `U \\u21a6 (M|_U \\u27f6 N|_U)`\\nwith the further-restriction maps. Functoriality is `restrictionMap_id` /\\n`restrictionMap_comp`. This is piece (a)+(b)+(c) of blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom`\\nat the abelian-group level; the `R(U)`-module refinement is added by `internalHom`. -/\\nnoncomputable def internalHomPresheaf\\n    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) :\\n    C\\u1d52\\u1d56 \\u2964 AddCommGrpCat.{max u uC vC} where\\n  obj X := AddCommGrpCat.of (restr X.unop M \\u27f6 restr X.unop N)\\n  map {X Y} f := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (restrictionMapAddHom f.unop M N)\\n  map_id X := by\\n    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext\\n    refine AddMonoidHom.ext fun \\u03c6 => ?_\\n    exact restrictionMap_id \\u03c6\\n  map_comp {X Y Z} f f' := by\\n    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext\\n    refine AddMonoidHom.ext fun \\u03c6 => ?_\\n    exact restrictionMap_comp f.unop f'.unop \\u03c6\\n\\n/-- **Semilinearity of `restrictionMap`** in the form consumed by\\n`PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf`: for `f : X \\u27f6 Y` in `C\\u1d52\\u1d56`, a global scalar\\n`r \\u2208 R(X)` and `m : M|_{X} \\u27f6 N|_{X}`, `restrictionMap f.unop (r \\u2022 m) =\\nR(f)(r) \\u2022 restrictionMap f.unop m`, where the `\\u2022`'s are the slice-value module\\nactions (`internalHomObjModule`). This is `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`'s\\ncompatibility datum; it follows from `restrictionMap_comp_hom` and\\n`restrictionMap_globalSMul`. -/\\nlemma restrictionMap_smul {X Y : C\\u1d52\\u1d56} (f : X \\u27f6 Y)\\n    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat))\\n    (r : (R.obj X : Type u)) (m : restr X.unop M \\u27f6 restr X.unop N) :\\n    restrictionMap f.unop\\n        (letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop M N; r \\u2022 m)\\n      = (letI := internalHomObjModule Y.unop M N;\\n          (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map f)) r \\u2022 restrictionMap f.unop m) := by\\n  change restrictionMap f.unop (m \\u226b globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal (restr X.unop N) r)\\n    = restrictionMap f.unop m\\n        \\u226b globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal (restr Y.unop N) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map f)) r)\\n  rw [restrictionMap_comp_hom, restrictionMap_globalSMul, Quiver.Hom.op_unop]\\n\\nend InternalHom\\n\\n/-! ### Assembly of the presheaf internal hom over a single-universe base\\n\\n`PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf` ties the underlying `Ab`-presheaf to the ground ring\\npresheaf `R`'s universe (`Type u`), but for a general base category the morphism groups\\n`M|_U \\u27f6 N|_U` live in `Type (max u uC vC)`. The two coincide exactly when the base\\ncategory is single-universe (`Category.{u, u}`), which is the case for the topological\\nsite `Opens X` underlying the structure sheaf of a scheme. The full presheaf internal\\nhom is therefore assembled in this specialised universe context. -/\\n\\nnamespace InternalHom\\n\\nsection Assembly\\n\\nvariable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R\\u2080 : D\\u1d52\\u1d56 \\u2964 CommRingCat.{u}}\\n\\n/-- **The presheaf internal hom `\\u210bom(M, N)`** (blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom`):\\nthe presheaf of `R`-modules assembled from the slice value modules\\n`internalHomObjModule` (a), the further-restriction maps `internalHomPresheaf` (b)(c),\\nand the semilinearity datum `restrictionMap_smul`, via `PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf`.\\nStated over a single-universe base (e.g. the topological site `Opens X`); see the\\nsection note for the universe constraint. -/\\nnoncomputable def internalHom\\n    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) :\\n    PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat) :=\\n  @PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf D _ (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)\\n    (internalHomPresheaf M N)\\n    (fun X => internalHomObjModule X.unop M N)\\n    (fun {_ _} f r m => restrictionMap_smul f M N r m)\\n\\nend Assembly\\n\\nend InternalHom\\n\\nnamespace InternalHom\\n\\nopen CategoryTheory Limits Opposite\\n\\nuniverse vC uC\\n\\nvariable {C : Type uC} [Category.{vC} C] {R : C\\u1d52\\u1d56 \\u2964 CommRingCat.{u}}\\n  {T : C} (hT : IsTerminal T)\\n\\n/-- **`termRingMap` at the terminal object itself is the identity.** The unique\\n`T \\u2192 T` is the identity, so the induced ring map `R(T) \\u2192 R(T)` is `id`. This is the\\nfact that the global-scalar action evaluated at the terminal section is the scalar\\nitself, used to prove the evaluation map of `internalHomEval` is `R(U)`-bilinear. -/\\nlemma termRingMap_terminal (f : (R.obj (op T) : Type u)) :\\n    (ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT (op T))) f = f := by\\n  have hid : hT.from T = \\ud835\\udfd9 T := hT.hom_ext _ _\\n  simp only [termRingMap, unop_op, hid, op_id, R.map_id]\\n  rfl\\n\\nend InternalHom\\n\\n/-! ### The presheaf dual `M^\\u2228 := \\u210bom(M, R)`\\n\\nThe presheaf dual is the internal hom into the monoidal unit (the structure presheaf\\nviewed as a module over itself), assembled in the same single-universe context as\\n`InternalHom.internalHom`. Blueprint `def:presheaf_dual`. -/\\n\\nsection Dual\\n\\nopen InternalHom Opposite\\n\\nvariable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R\\u2080 : D\\u1d52\\u1d56 \\u2964 CommRingCat.{u}}\\n\\n/-- **The presheaf dual `M^\\u2228 := \\u210bom(M, R)`** (blueprint `def:presheaf_dual`): the\\ninternal hom into the monoidal unit `\\ud835\\udfd9_` (the structure presheaf `R` viewed as a module\\nover itself / the regular representation). The value\\n`M^\\u2228(U) = ModuleCat.of (R(U)) (M|_U \\u27f6 R|_U)` is the `R(U)`-module of `R|_U`-linear\\nfunctionals on `M|_U`, the presheaf-of-modules analogue of the fixed-ring linear dual\\n`Module.Dual R M = (M \\u27f6 R)` that serves as the `\\u2297`-inverse of an invertible module.\\nProject-local: Mathlib has the fixed-ring dual but no `PresheafOfModules`-level dual for\\nthe varying structure sheaf. -/\\nnoncomputable def dual\\n    (M : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) :\\n    PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat) :=\\n  InternalHom.internalHom M (\\ud835\\udfd9_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)))\\n\\n/-- The evaluation functional `\\u03c6 \\u21a6 (s \\u21a6 \\u03c6(s))` of a dual section `\\u03c6`, cast to an\\n`R\\u2080.obj X`-linear map `M(X) \\u2192 R(X)` (the over-category ring at the terminal section is\\ndefinitionally `R\\u2080.obj X`). Helper for `internalHomEvalApp`. -/\\nnoncomputable def evalLin\\n    (M : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) (X : D\\u1d52\\u1d56)\\n    (\\u03c6 : (dual M).obj X) :\\n    (M.obj X : Type u) \\u2192\\u2097[(R\\u2080.obj X : Type u)] (R\\u2080.obj X : Type u) :=\\n  ((\\u03c6 : restr X.unop M \\u27f6 restr X.unop\\n      (\\ud835\\udfd9_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)))).app\\n        (op (Over.mk (\\ud835\\udfd9 X.unop)))).hom\\n\\n/-- `evalLin` is additive in the dual section `\\u03c6` (the dual-section addition is the\\ncategorical `Hom`-addition, on which `app`/`hom`-application is definitionally additive). -/\\nlemma evalLin_add\\n    (M : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R\\u2080 \\u22d9 The identity to\n  prove is therefore a paste of \\emph{four commuting squares}, conjugated throughout by\n  the pullback pseudofunctoriality isomorphism \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f\\). Unlike\n  the \\emph{naturality} paste of \\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural} (D1$'$), this is\n  a \\emph{composition}-coherence paste: the left-hand side is the composite-morphism\n  (\\(h \\circ f\\)) instance, while the right-hand side interleaves the \\(f\\)-instance\n  (transported by \\(h^{*}\\)) with the \\(h\\)-instance (on the pulled-back modules\n  \\(f^{*}M, f^{*}N\\)).\n\n  We record \\emph{why} the unit-comparison mirror does not apply. The unit coherence\n  \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} succeeds because\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\\) is, by definition, an adjunction transpose, so its\n  composition coherence is obtained by transposing across the pullback--pushforward\n  adjunction. The proof instead\n  pastes the four squares directly.\n\n  \\emph{Sq2 (the \\(\\delta\\) core).} The presheaf-level oplax comparison \\(\\delta\\) of the\n  \\emph{composite} pullback decomposes by the Mathlib oplax-monoidal coherence\n  \\(\\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\\_}\\delta\\), once the composite presheaf pullback\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{h \\circ f}\\) is identified with the composite functor\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{f} \\,\\circ\\, \\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{h}\\) through the\n  Mathlib presheaf coherence \\(\\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\\). This\n  identification rests on the ring-map reconciliation\n  \\[\n    (\\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\\,(h \\circ f)).\\mathrm{hom}\n      \\;=\\; \\varphi'_{f} \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n      (\\mathtt{Opens.map}\\,f.\\mathrm{base})^{\\mathrm{op}}.\\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\\,\\varphi'_{h},\n  \\]\n  which is \\emph{definitional}: although the two sides are presented in functor categories\n  that nominally differ by the equality \\(\\mathtt{Opens.map}\\,(h.\\mathrm{base} \\circ\n  f.\\mathrm{base}) = \\mathtt{Opens.map}\\,f.\\mathrm{base} \\,\\circ\\, \\mathtt{Opens.map}\\,\n  h.\\mathrm{base}\\) (\\(\\mathtt{Opens.map\\_comp}\\)), this equality and the underlying\n  scheme-composition data already hold up to definitional equality at default\n  transparency, so the reconciliation is definitional\n  (\\(\\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom\\_comp\\_hom\\_reconcile}\\)). The genuine content of Sq2 is\n  therefore not the reconciliation but the monoidality of \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) itself,\n  isolated as Sq2b below.\n\n  \\emph{Sq2b (monoidality of \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) --- the genuinely new ingredient).}\n  The decomposition in Sq2 reduces the \\(\\delta\\)-core to a single structural fact, absent\n  from Mathlib: the connecting isomorphism\n  \\[\n    \\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,\\varphi'_{h} \\;:\\;\n      \\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{f} \\,\\circ\\, \\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{h}\n      \\;\\cong\\; \\mathtt{pullback}\\,\n        \\bigl(\\varphi'_{f} \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n          (\\mathtt{Opens.map}\\,f.\\mathrm{base})^{\\mathrm{op}}.\\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\\,\n          \\varphi'_{h}\\bigr)\n  \\]\n  is a \\emph{monoidal} natural isomorphism between the oplax-monoidal functors\n  \\(\\bigl(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{f} \\circ \\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{h},\\,\n  \\mathtt{comp\\_}\\delta\\bigr)\\) and \\(\\bigl(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,(\\varphi'_{f}\\mathbin{;}\n  \\cdots),\\,\\delta\\bigr)\\). Writing \\(\\delta\\) for the oplax tensorator of the single\n  composite pullback and \\(\\mathtt{comp\\_}\\delta = (\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{h})\n  .\\mathrm{map}\\,\\delta^{\\varphi'_{f}} \\mathbin{;} \\delta^{\\varphi'_{h}}\\) for the\n  composite tensorator of the two-step pullback, monoidality is the identity\n  \\[\n    \\delta_{M,N}\n      \\;=\\; (\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,\\varphi'_{h}).\\mathrm{inv}_{M\\otimes N}\n      \\;\\mathbin{;}\\; (\\mathtt{comp\\_}\\delta)_{M,N} \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n      \\bigl((\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,\\varphi'_{h}).\\mathrm{hom}_{M}\n        \\otimes (\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,\\varphi'_{h}).\\mathrm{hom}_{N}\\bigr).\n  \\]\n  This is precisely the \\(\\delta\\)-twin of the unit coherence\n  \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}, and it is proved by the \\emph{same} mate-calculus\n  argument with the unit comparison \\(\\eta\\) replaced by the tensorator \\(\\delta\\). This last residual --- isolated as the lemma\n  \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp\\_lax\\_}\\mu\\) --- is the \\emph{right-adjoint twin} of the\n  \\(\\delta\\)-coherence: it is precisely the assertion that the pseudofunctoriality\n  isomorphism \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\\) is \\emph{monoidal} for the lax tensorators\n  \\(\\mu\\), i.e.\\ that comparing the lax-\\(\\mu\\) structure of the single composite\n  \\(\\mathtt{pushforward}\\) with the composite of the two individual lax-\\(\\mu\\) structures\n  agrees across \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\\). Two points must be stated precisely.\n\n  First, the mate-calculus reduction proceeds as follows: the transpose-and-inject\n  opening, the \\(\\delta = \\mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\\bigl((\\eta\\otimes\\eta)\\mathbin{;}\\mu\\bigr)\\)\n  pivot, the \\(\\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\\_pullbackComp\\_inv}\\) transport across the adjunctions\n  (with \\(\\mathtt{unit\\_conjugateEquiv}\\) and \\(\\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\\_unit\\_app}\\)), and\n  the two-argument \\(\\mathtt{tensorHom}\\)/\\(\\delta_{\\mathrm{natural}}\\) bookkeeping --- which\n  follows the template of Mathlib's\n  \\(\\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.isMonoidal\\_comp}\\) (the canonical ``a composite of\n  monoidal adjunctions is monoidal'') --- together reduce Sq2b to the single residual\n  \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp\\_lax\\_}\\mu\\). This is the content of\n  \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}'s \\(\\delta\\)-mirror.\n\n  Second, this residual \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp\\_lax\\_}\\mu\\) is discharged by a short\n  \\emph{sectionwise pure-tensor collapse}, not by a change-of-rings calculation. On the\n  strongly-monoidal pushforward objects the pushforward tensorator \\(\\mu\\) reduces\n  \\emph{definitionally} to the lighter \\(\\mathtt{restrictScalars}\\) tensorator \\(\\mu\\) (the\n  identity \\(\\mathtt{pushforward\\_}\\mu\\mathtt{\\_eq}\\), which holds by reflexivity); after\n  this reduction, evaluated on a pure tensor \\(m \\otimes n\\) every\n  \\(\\mathtt{restrictScalars}\\) tensorator is the identity (Mathlib's\n  \\(\\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars\\_}\\mu\\mathtt{\\_tmul}\\)), so both sides of the\n  interchange collapse to the same pure tensor \\(m \\otimes n\\); no change-of-rings\n  construction is required. This closes the Sq2b obligation.\n\n  Crucially, this sub-step is stated at the \\(\\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\\) level --- about\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'\\) with \\(\\varphi'\\) the ring map\n  \\((\\mathcal{S}_{0}\\mathbin{;}\\mathtt{forget}_{2}) \\to f^{\\mathrm{op}}\\mathbin{;}\n  (\\mathcal{R}_{0}\\mathbin{;}\\mathtt{forget}_{2})\\) carried by \\(\\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\\)\n  --- rather than at the scheme level, where the composite factorisation is immediate from\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) and the ring-map reconciliation is definitional.\n\n  The observation above --- that \\(\\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\\) is not an adjunction\n  transpose --- concerns the \\emph{full} four-fold composite and does not constrain\n  Sq2b: the inner tensorator \\(\\delta\\) genuinely \\emph{is} a transpose\n  (\\(\\mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\\) of \\((\\eta\\otimes\\eta)\\mathbin{;}\\mu\\)), so for Sq2b\n  specifically the \\(\\eta\\to\\delta\\) mirror of \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} is the\n  correct route.\n\n  \\emph{Sq1 (sheafification \\(\\leftrightarrow\\) pullback) --- the sole open ingredient.} The\n  composition coherence of \\(\\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\\) across\n  \\(h \\circ f\\) --- the analog of \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) for the\n  ``sheafify-then-pullback'' natural isomorphism --- is \\emph{absent from Mathlib} and is\n  supplied as a named, private project sub-lemma\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\\_comp}\\). For a presheaf \\(P\\) over \\(X\\), writing\n  \\(a_X, a_Z\\) for the sheafifications and \\(\\mathtt{sheafCompPb} =\n  \\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\\), it asserts\n  \\[\n    ((\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\\,(h \\circ f)).\\mathrm{app}\\,P).\\mathrm{hom}\n      \\;=\\;\n      (\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f).\\mathrm{inv}_{a_X P} \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n      h^{*}\\bigl(((\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\\,f).\\mathrm{app}\\,P).\\mathrm{hom}\\bigr) \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n      ((\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\\,h).\\mathrm{app}\\,\n        (\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,P)).\\mathrm{hom} \\;\\mathbin{;}\\;\n      a_Z.\\mathrm{map}\\bigl(\n        (\\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,\\varphi'_{h}).\\mathrm{hom}_P\n      \\bigr).\n  \\]\n  It is proved by the \\(\\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\\) mate calculus: both \\(\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\\)\n  isomorphisms are \\(\\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\\) of composite adjunctions, so transposing the\n  identity under the composite adjunction for \\(h \\circ f\\) and evaluating the left-hand side\n  by the mate identity \\(\\mathtt{homEquiv\\_leftAdjointUniq\\_hom\\_app}\\) reduces it to a\n  unit-level identity; the two \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) factors are then transported across\n  the adjunction units --- the \\(\\delta\\)-free twin of\n  \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.\n\n  \\emph{The reduced tail goal.} After peeling the leading pseudofunctoriality factor\n  \\((\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f).\\mathrm{inv}\\), the remaining obligation is a unit\n  identity for the two-layer composite adjunction (presheaf pullback--pushforward adjunction\n  composed with the sheafification adjunction), isolated as the named sub-lemma\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\\_comp\\_tail}\\). Writing \\(B_{\\varphi}\\) for the\n  sheaf-level composite adjunction of \\cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta_general} and\n  \\(\\mathtt{PresheafPullback}_f\\) for the presheaf-level pullback along \\(f\\), the assembly\n  proceeds in the following ordered steps, mirroring the already-closed\n  \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (whose proof is the \\(\\delta\\)-free analog of this one,\n  exactly one sheafification layer down):\n  \\begin{enumerate}\n    \\item[(a)] \\emph{Strip the outer identity wrapper.} The tail goal carries an outer\n      \\(\\mathtt{restrictScalars}(\\mathbf{1})\\) change-of-rings wrapper coming from the\n      identity ring map; this restriction-of-scalars-along-identity factor is removed first,\n      so that the genuine comparison factors are exposed.\n    \\item[(b)] \\emph{Distribute the right-hand sheaf composite under \\(\\mathtt{forget}\\).} The\n      right-hand side is the sheaf-level composite carrying the two sub-comparison factors\n      \\(R_1\\) (the \\(f\\)-layer) and \\(R_5\\) (the \\(h\\)-layer). Pushing the forgetful functor\n      \\(\\mathtt{forget}\\) (sheaf \\(\\to\\) presheaf) through the composite separates these two\n      factors \\emph{without} disturbing the left-hand composite unit \\(B_{h\\circ f}.\\mathrm{unit}\\),\n      which is left intact for the final reassembly.\n    \\item[(c)] \\emph{Recover the sub-comparison units.} Apply\n      \\cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta_general}\n      (\\(\\mathtt{leftAdjointUniqUnitEta\\_app}\\), the \\(P\\)-general step-1 brick) to rewrite\n      \\(R_1\\) as the composite-adjunction unit \\(B_f.\\mathrm{unit}.\\mathrm{app}\\,P\\) and\n      \\(R_5\\) as \\(B_h.\\mathrm{unit}.\\mathrm{app}\\,(\\mathtt{PresheafPullback}_f\\,P)\\). This is\n      precisely the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints characterisation\n      \\(\\mathtt{homEquiv\\_leftAdjointUniq\\_hom\\_app}\\), now used in its \\(P\\)-general form.\n    \\item[(d)] \\emph{Slide the \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\\) comparison past the recovered\n      units.} The sheaf-level comparison \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\\,h\\,f\\) is moved across the\n      two recovered units \\(B_f.\\mathrm{unit}\\) and \\(B_h.\\mathrm{unit}\\) by its naturality.\n    \\item[(e)] \\emph{Reassemble the composite unit.} Expanding\n      \\(B_{h\\circ f}.\\mathrm{unit}\\) by the composite-adjunction unit formula\n      \\(\\mathtt{comp\\_unit\\_app}\\) and applying \\(\\mathtt{unit\\_naturality}\\) identifies the\n      slid expression of step~(d) with the left-hand composite unit, closing the tail.\n  \\end{enumerate}\n\n  \\emph{The precise binding obligation.} The one place where the assembly is not yet a\n  mechanical paste is the bridge between the \\emph{presheaf}-level left-hand data (the\n  presheaf pushforward \\(\\mathtt{pushforward}^{\\mathrm{pre}}\\) and the composite unit\n  \\(B_{h\\circ f}.\\mathrm{unit}\\)) and the \\emph{sheaf}-level right-hand factors \\(R_1, R_5\\)\n  wrapped in \\(\\mathtt{forget}\\). Crossing this boundary requires the compatibility\n  \\[\n    \\mathtt{forget} \\circ \\mathtt{pushforward}^{\\mathrm{sheaf}}\n      \\;=\\;\n    \\mathtt{pushforward}^{\\mathrm{pre}} \\circ \\mathtt{forget},\n  \\]\n  interacting with the recovered \\(B_f\\)- and \\(B_h\\)-units of step~(c). This compatibility is\n  the natural unit to isolate as its own named sub-lemma \\emph{before} the assembly: once it\n  is in hand, steps (a)--(e) become a mechanical paste, whereas without it the presheaf- and\n  sheaf-level pushforwards cannot be aligned at the recovered units. The remainder of the tail\n  is bookkeeping around this single compatibility.\n\n  This is the sheafification-laden analog of \\cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}; unlike\n  that lemma it is not definitional sectionwise, because the adjunctions involved are\n  composite rather than single transposes. The outer Sq1 lemma\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\\_comp}\\) \\emph{consumes} this tail: it peels the leading\n  \\((\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f).\\mathrm{inv}\\) factor and then invokes\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\\_comp\\_tail}\\) for the residual unit identity.\n  \\emph{Warning:} transposing the \\emph{whole} tail back under the composite adjunction --- as\n  opposed to recovering the two sub-units individually via step~(c) and reassembling --- is\n  \\emph{circular} (verified): it returns the very identity one set out to prove, so the\n  step-(c) recovery of the individual \\(R_1, R_5\\) units is essential.\n\n  \\emph{Sq3 (unit-iso transport).} The monoidal-unit sheafification isomorphism\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\\) is carried through the same \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\)\n  identification used for Sq2.\n\n  \\emph{Sq4 (the connecting iso).} The scheme-level composition coherence of\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\) relates \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\,(h \\circ f)\\,M\\) to\n  \\(h^{*}\\bigl(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\,f\\,M\\bigr)\\),\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\,h\\,(f^{*}M)\\), and the pseudofunctoriality component\n  \\((\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f)_M\\); it produces the final\n  \\(\\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\\bigl((\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f)_M,\n  (\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\,h\\,f)_N\\bigr)\\) factor. It is \\emph{absent from Mathlib and not yet\n  built}; but \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\) factors through \\(\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\\) (as\n  \\(\\mathtt{sheafCompPb}^{-1}\\) followed by the pullback of a counit), so its composition\n  coherence reduces to that of Sq1 together with the pseudofunctoriality of the counit ---\n  i.e.\\ Sq4 becomes a short corollary once Sq1 is in hand.\n\n  This Sq4 coherence is a \\emph{standalone named obligation}, to be built separately and not\n  folded into Sq1: it is the \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\) \\emph{composition-coherence} sub-lemma\n  --- the naturality across the connecting-object boundary where the abstract pullback's\n  \\(\\mathtt{.val}\\) presentation meets the helper-\\(\\mathtt{.obj}\\) presentation --- that is\n  consumed inside \\(\\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap\\_restrict}\\). The prover should treat it as its own\n  brick (a corollary of Sq1, but with its own statement and its own connecting-object\n  bookkeeping), not as a step inlined in the proof of Sq1.\n\n  The two remaining ingredients are Sq1 (\\(\\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\\_comp}\\)) and,\n  downstream of it, Sq4: the monoidality Sq2b (\\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp\\_}\\delta\\) together with\n  \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp\\_lax\\_}\\mu\\)) follows from the mate-calculus reduction above, the\n  Sq3 unit-iso transport follows from the same \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) identification, and\n  the Sq2 ring-map reconciliation is definitional. Sq4 reduces to Sq1\n  via the \\(\\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\\) factorisation, so Sq1 is the open target.\n  The adjunction-mate bridge \\(\\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\\_pullbackComp\\_inv}\\) (relating\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullbackComp}\\) for the left adjoints to \\(\\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\\) for the\n  right adjoints) is the device used in Sq2b and Sq4 to transport the pseudofunctoriality\n  coherence across the adjunctions.\n\n  The four squares do \\emph{not} paste row-by-row. Because \\(S_1^{h}\\) acts on\n  \\(\\bigl((\\mathtt{pullback}\\,f).\\mathrm{obj}\\,M\\bigr) \\otimes\n  \\bigl((\\mathtt{pullback}\\,f).\\mathrm{obj}\\,N\\bigr)\\), \\emph{not} on\n  \\(\\mathtt{pullback}\\,\\varphi'_{f}\\,(M \\otimes N)\\), the factors must first be slid past one\n  another by \\(\\delta_{\\mathrm{natural}}\\) and the naturality of the intervening natural\n  transformations before assembly, exactly as in the D1$'$ naturality paste of\n  \\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural}. With Sq1 in hand, the Sq2 oplax decomposition, the\n  Sq3 unit-iso transport, and the Sq4 connecting-iso coherence assemble the four squares into\n  the displayed identity. The base-change-square consequence (the Remark) is then the\n  specialisation \\(h := j'\\) obtained by equating the two factorisations \\(j' \\mathbin{;} f =\n  g \\mathbin{;} j\\); the iff-clause is immediate, an isomorphism being preserved and reflected\n  by the pseudofunctoriality isomorphism it is conjugated by.\n",

{'id': 'def:line_bundle_on_product', 'type': 'definition', 'title': '', 'chapter': 'Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve', 'statement': "ok\n[Line bundle on a product]\n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \n  \\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,\n  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''\n  (cf.\\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}\n  Let \\(k\\) be a base field, \\(C\\) a \\(k\\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper\n  geometrically integral curve), and \\(T\\) a \\(k\\)-scheme. A\n  \\emph{line bundle on the relative curve \\(C \\times_k T\\)} is an invertible\n  \\(\\mathcal{O}_{C \\times_k T}\\)-module, that is, an\n  \\(\\mathcal{O}_{C \\times_k T}\\)-module \\(\\mathcal{L}\\) such that there exists an\n  open cover \\(\\{U_i\\}\\) of \\(C \\times_k T\\) on which each restriction\n  \\(\\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if\n  they are isomorphic as \\(\\mathcal{O}_{C \\times_k T}\\)-modules. The set of\n  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the\n  \\emph{Picard group}), denoted\n  \\(\\Pic(C \\times_k T)\\). In Kleiman's notation with \\(X = C\\) and \\(S = \\Spec k\\),\n  this is exactly the value\n  \\(\\Pic_C(T) = \\Pic(C \\times_k T)\\) of the absolute Picard functor.", 'uses': [], 'lean_name': 'AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct', 'proved': True, 'mathlib_ok': False, 'proof_tex': '', 'lean_source': 'def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=\n  { M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M }', 'proof_size_lean': 140, 'has_sorry': False, 'tex_file': 'chapters/Picard_LineBundlePullback.tex', 'lean_file': 'AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean', 'dep_count': 0, 'rdep_count': 1, 'descendant_count': 2, 'proof_size_tex': None, 'effort_local': 0, 'proof_size_tex_total': None, 'proof_size_lean_total': 140, 'effort_total': 0}
