/home/archon/proj/Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso/.archon/logs/iter-004/plan.jsonl:5:{"ts": "2026-06-09T07:23:38.023591Z", "event": "tool_result", "content": "/home/archon/proj/Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso\ntotal 212\ndrwxr-xr-x 11 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 22:37 .\ndrwxr-xr-x 14 archon archon  4096 Jun  9 13:35 ..\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  1349 Jun  8 20:35 .env\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon 17270 Jun  9 09:56 AGENTS.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon   689 May 27 14:15 ARCHON_MEMORY.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  4463 May 14 10:23 MULTILANE.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  6584 Jun  8 22:36 PROGRESS.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  5321 Jun  9 10:21 PROJECT_STATUS.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  4929 Jun  8 20:54 STRATEGY.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon   530 Jun  9 10:20 TO_USER.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  2734 Jun  9 13:37 USER_HINTS.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon     6 Jun  9 13:35 VERSION\ndrwxr-xr-x  2 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 19:49 analogies\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  8695 Jun  9 13:39 config.json\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon 12987 Jun  8 19:46 extract-manifest.json\ndrwxr-xr-x  8 archon archon  4096 Jun  9 15:23 git-dir\ndrwxr-xr-x  6 archon archon  4096 Jun  9 15:23 iter\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon 57414 Jun  8 20:39 last_lake_build.log\ndrwxr-xr-x  6 archon archon  4096 Jun  9 15:23 logs\ndrwxr-xr-x  3 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 20:35 prompts\ndrwxr-xr-x  4 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 20:35 proof-journal\ndrwxr-xr-x  2 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 19:05 prover-modes\ndrwxr-xr-x  2 archon archon  4096 Jun  8 19:05 subagents\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon   153 Jun  9 10:10 sync_leanok-state.json\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon     0 May 14 10:23 task_done.md\n-rw-r--r--  1 archon archon  2481 Jun  8 22:37 task_pending.md\ndrwxr-xr-x  2 archon archon  4096 Jun  9 13:37 task_results\n# Archon Project\n\nYou are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.\n\n## Priority Rule\n\nWhen instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:\n\n- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.\n- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.\n- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.\n\n## Skills\n\n- **archon-lean4** \u2014 under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.\n\n## Tools\n\nProject tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts \u2014 that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.\n\nTwo always-present scripts:\n\n- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** \u2014 call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) \u2014 check via `env | grep -E \"DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI\"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog \u2014 when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there \u2014 or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.\n- **`archon-subagent.py`** \u2014 the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent \u2014 see \"Subagents\" below).\n\n## Subagents\n\nSubagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter \u2014 `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` \u2014 followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).\n\n**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.\n\n**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):\n\n```\npython3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \\\n  --name <subagent-name> \\\n  --slug <kebab-slug> \\\n  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \\\n  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple\n```\n\nThe wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.\n\n**Treat each dispatch as blocking \u2014 wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately \u2014 that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.\n\n**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip \u2014 when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions \u2014 read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.\n\n## Key Files & Permissions\n\nAll Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row \u2014 they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See \"Subagent permissions\" after the table.\n\n| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |\n|------|------|--------|--------|------|\n| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\n| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | \u2014 | \u2014 | read |\n| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |\n| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | \u2014 | \u2014 | write |\n| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\n| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\n| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |\n| `proof-journal/` | read | \u2014 | **write** | read |\n| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | \u2014 | **write** (Knowledge Base only \u2014 session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |\n| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | \u2014 | read (recent window injected) | read |\n| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | \u2014 | **write** (this iter only) | read |\n| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | \u2014 | read | read |\n| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |\n| `.lean` files | \u2014 | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | \u2014 | write (via comments) |\n| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\\lean{...}` corrections \u2014 NOT `\\leanok`) | read |\n| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | \u2014 | \u2014 | read |\n| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |\n\n### Subagent permissions\n\nEach subagent ships YAML frontmatter:\n\n- `write_domain: \"<glob>\"` \u2014 file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).\n- `read_only: true | false` \u2014 when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.\n- `can_spawn: true | false` \u2014 when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.\n\nThe dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).\n\n**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.\n\n## Protected Declarations\n\n`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:\n\n- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.\n- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.\n- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.\n\n## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary\n\nTwo semantic markers:\n\n- `\\leanok` \u2014 statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.\n- `\\mathlibok` \u2014 statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.\n\nNo marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.\n\n**`\\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\\leanok` as routine bookkeeping \u2014 leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).\n\n`\\mathlibok`, `\\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.\n\n## User Interaction\n\nThe user provides hints in two places:\n\n- **Strategic hints** \u2192 `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as \"no hints\" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.\n- **File-specific hints** \u2192 `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.\n\nArchon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** \u2014 a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:\n\n- **Concise**: the whole file stays \u2264 2\u20133 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.\n- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.\n- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write \"awaiting your decision\", an options menu, a \"where to reply\", or \"default to X if no reply\" \u2014 anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.\n\n## Agent Roles\n\n### Plan Agent\n\n- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.\n- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not \"go read\" those files.\n- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.\n- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.\n- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.\n- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` \u2014 the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.\n- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers \u2014 `\\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.\n- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.\n\n### Prover Agent\n\n- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).\n- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt \u2014 read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.\n- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.\n- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.\n- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned \u2014 never edit another agent's file.\n- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.\n- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.\n\n### Subagents\n\nSubagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see \"Subagents\" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at \u2014 never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state \u2014 and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).\n\n### Review Agent\n\n- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.\n- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.\n- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).\n- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only \u2014 session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).\n- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers \u2014 `\\mathlibok`, `\\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\\notready` removal. Normally leave `\\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.\n- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.\n\n### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)\n\nThese run automatically between phases:\n\n- **Iter sidecar init** \u2014 creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.\n- **`sync_leanok`** \u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.\n- **`blueprint-doctor`** \u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\\ref` / `\\uses` / `\\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.\n\n==== PLAN PROMPT ====\n# Plan Agent\n\nYou are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).\n\n## Iteration number\n\nYour invocation prompt contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical counter \u2014 written to `logs/iter-NNN/`, stamped into commit messages, and exposed to subagent tools as `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment.\n\nThe session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent.\n\n## What the loop has already done for you\n\nEverything below is pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do NOT need to \"go read\" any of these files \u2014 acting on them is enough.\n\n- **User hints** captured from `USER_HINTS.md` (cleared after your phase succeeds).\n- **Blueprint-doctor findings** from the prior iter (orphan chapters, broken `\\ref`/`\\uses`, new axioms).\n- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).\n- **Subagent catalog** (every enabled subagent's name + description + dispatcher rules \u2014 the authoritative roster for this iter; do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).\n- **References summary** from `references/summary.md` when present.\n\n## Your Job\n\n1. Read the injected blocks above.\n2. Collect prover results from `task_results/<file>.md`; merge findings into `task_pending.md` (attempts) and `task_done.md` (resolved). Clear processed result files. (Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the loop.)\n3. Read `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` for context \u2014 do not repeat documented dead ends.\n4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` for the latest session's `summary.md` + `recommendations.md`. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if present.\n5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** before writing prover objectives or invoking subagents (see \"Long-arc Strategy\" below).\n6. For each active task: completed? feasible? if not, why? does a subagent in your catalog help?\n7. Trust the loop's deterministic sorry-count + commit metadata. Spot-check independently only when a prover's self-report is internally inconsistent.\n8. Replace unreasonable tasks (impossible / wrong approach) with corrected plans in `PROGRESS.md`.\n9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see \"Blueprint chapters\" below). Keep blueprint and Lean consistent.\n10. Optionally invoke subagents (see \"Subagent delegation\" below). **Your injected catalog is the authoritative roster \u2014 dispatch only subagents that appear there** (no subagent is enabled by default in the loop; the user opts in via `.archon/config.json`). Mandatory ones in your catalog are tagged `[MANDATORY]` \u2014 you MUST dispatch them this phase. When present in your catalog, `strategy-auditor` validates strategic routes against reference PDFs, `blueprint-clean` enforces blueprint purity, and `lean-scaffolder` sets up Lean files and conveys implementation hints \u2014 but if a name below isn't in your catalog this iter, it isn't available; don't try to dispatch it.\n11. Set self-contained objectives for the next prover round in `PROGRESS.md`.\n12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to, stop and return to coordination.\n13. Detect and address project-wide critical issues (wrong definitions, false statements, flawed strategies, axioms) \u2014 even when long-present.\n\n## Write permissions and boundaries\n\nYou may write `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`, `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`. You must NOT edit `.lean` files, `task_results/` files, or `USER_HINTS.md` (the loop manages that one for you).\n\n**You decide; you never wait.** The loop is autonomous \u2014 it often runs unattended overnight, and no one may read a question for many iters. So every strategy-level choice (which route, whether to amend a signature, which option closes a blocker fastest) is YOURS to make: pick the best option on the evidence, commit to it, and dispatch provers on it THIS iter. Never skip prover dispatch or idle an iter waiting for a human reply. The user steers by adding hints to `USER_HINTS.md` *if and when* they disagree \u2014 treat that as an asynchronous override you'll honour the next iter it appears, never a gate you wait on.\n\n**Notification channels** (these inform the user; they do NOT block you):\n- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` \u2014 full rationale for the decision you made.\n- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** \u2014 skip prover dispatch ONLY for a MECHANICAL hard gate (no ready sorries; every objective blocked by a failed upstream build) \u2014 NEVER for a pending user decision. When a mechanical gate fires, write the marker `(no prover dispatch this iter \u2014 see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)`. The plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional.\n- **TO_USER.md** \u2014 a *persistent* shared notice board (you, the provers, and review all maintain it; it is no longer auto-cleared each iter). You MAY write it directly to surface a user-facing FYI \u2014 the decision you made + how to override it, or a genuine blocker needing user action (see \"Stuck routes\" below). Discipline: keep the **whole file \u2264 2\u20133 short bullets**; before adding, read it and **delete any bullet no longer true at this point** (resolved blocker, overtaken decision); state things as *made*/*worked-around*, never as a pending question. It is a notice board, not a question queue \u2014 the loop never waits.\n\n**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on \u2014 nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced there. Off-limits files belong in a separate section.\n\n**Blueprint gate** (before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`): the corresponding blueprint chapter must be complete + correct per the catalog's latest blueprint-review status. The chapter for F is the one declaring `% archon:covers ... F ...` if any (a consolidated chapter that blueprints several files), else the 1:1 `Foo/Bar.lean \u2192 Foo_Bar.tex` slug; a covered chapter's verdict gates every file it lists. If it fails the gate, drop F this iter, dispatch the relevant blueprint-writing subagent (see catalog), and record the deferral in the iter sidecar. **Blueprint purity:** After significant edits, when `blueprint-clean` is in your catalog, dispatch it to strip out Lean syntax, fix missing quotes, and remove project-history verbosity before provers run. **Same-iter fast path:** on a pivot iter where you rewrite chapter C and `lake build` then goes green, you do NOT have to wait a whole iter for the next mandatory review \u2014 re-dispatch the blueprint-reviewer *scoped to C alone*; if it returns C complete + correct with no must-fix, add C's files to the objectives and send a prover THIS iter. See the blueprint-reviewer's HARD GATE section for the exact rule. The fast path never bypasses the gate: a fresh complete+correct verdict is still required (a green build alone is not enough).\n\n**Diligence**: never choose laziness. Even when the task spans many iters / LOC, dive in, restructure, fill gaps \u2014 the user sees your iter / LOC estimations in STRATEGY.md and expects effort that matches them.\n\n**No new axioms.** If axioms are already present, remove them. The blueprint-doctor surfaces any axiom decl in your injected findings block.\n\n## Boundary: mathematical intent, not Lean syntax\n\nYour output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.\n\n- **You MAY** use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists*.\n- **You MUST NOT** use `lean_run_code` to validate proof bodies, search tactic sequences, or type-check expressions. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop \u2014 that is the prover's job.\n\nWhen your plan recipe suggests a Mathlib lemma, tag it: `[verified]` (you confirmed via search this iter), `[expected]` (guessing by naming conventions \u2014 prover treats as hint, not fact), `[gap]` (you verified it doesn't exist). Past iters' verification does NOT carry forward; Mathlib bumps rename and remove things.\n\n## Protected declarations\n\n`archon-protected.yaml` lists the mathematician's read-only surface. No agent may modify protected signatures. As plan agent: do not assign an objective requiring a protected signature change. Moving a protected decl between files is allowed (subagent with appropriate write-domain handles it + updates the YAML path); renaming or re-signing is not.\n\n## References\n\n`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly \u2014 don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.\n\nThe summary's `How to read (confirmed working)` column is a living log. After you successfully ingest a file, fill in or correct that file's row with what actually worked: `Read` (and any options, e.g. `pages: \"1-5\"` for long PDFs), or the exact shell command you fell back to (e.g. `pdftotext file.pdf -`). If `Read` fails on a PDF with a missing-`pdftoppm` error, note the fallback you used; don't make the next agent rediscover it.\n\n## Blueprint chapters\n\nInformal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` \u2192 `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level \u2014 not sketches.\n\n**Consolidated chapters.** When the math for several Lean files is most naturally written as one chapter (and the sibling chapters would just be thin pointers), declare the coverage explicitly at the top of the consolidated chapter:\n\n```latex\n% archon:covers RigidityKbar.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebraS3.lean\n```\n\n(whitespace- or comma-separated, repeatable across lines). The prover-dispatch gate then treats that one chapter as the blueprint for all listed files, and the blueprint doctor lints the declaration (covered file must exist; no file covered by two chapters). Without a `covers:` line the strict 1:1 slug mapping applies.\n\nBefore assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:\n\n```latex\n\\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]\n  \\label{thm:some_label}\n  \\lean{namespace.theorem_name}\n  \\uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}\n  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne.pdf)\n  % SOURCE QUOTE: \"A morphism $f: X \\to Y$ of schemes locally of finite\n  % type is said to be smooth at $x \\in X$ if there exist an open affine\n  % neighborhood $V = \\Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood\n  % $U = \\Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \\subset V$ such that ...\"\n  \\textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}\n  Informal statement, in the project's notation.\n\\end{theorem}\n% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: \"Proof. We may assume $Y = \\Spec B$ and\n% $X = \\Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism\n% $\\varphi: B \\to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ...\"\n\\begin{proof}\n  \\uses{thm:another_result}\n  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Detail enough to formalize.\n\\end{proof}\n```\n\n**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.\n\n**Citation discipline.** Every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material MUST include:\n\n1. A `% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment naming **(a)** the citation pointer \u2014 source identifier, section / theorem / definition number, page when available \u2014 AND **(b)** the local file under `references/` it was read from. Format: `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>)`. The `(read from \u2026)` parenthetical is mandatory \u2014 it documents which local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote on the next line. Name the actual source file you quoted from \u2014 the downloaded PDF/TeX (`references/<slug>.pdf`, `references/<slug>.tex`) when one exists, not its pointer `.md` index card (which holds only a citation + contents map, never quotable text).\n2. A `% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment containing the **verbatim text** of the cited statement. Verbatim means:\n   - **In the source's original language** (French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's pre-EGA work, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, \u2026). Do NOT translate.\n   - **Original notation preserved character-by-character**. If the source writes $\\mathcal{O}_X^*$ where the project writes $\\mathcal{O}_X^\\times$, the quote keeps $\\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The visible project-notation restatement happens AFTER the quote, in the prose body.\n   - **Every word and every symbol preserved**. No paraphrase. No abbreviation. No \"obvious\" omissions. If a word feels redundant in the source, it still goes in the quote.\n   - Long quotes are fine \u2014 LaTeX comments don't render in the PDF and don't bloat the typeset output.\n3. A visible `\\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line as the first line of the block's prose (renders in the PDF so the human reader sees the citation at a glance without grep).\n\nFor **proof blocks**: add a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). It contains the **verbatim original-language proof** from the source \u2014 same rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:` (original language, original notation, every word). The informal proof body that follows inside `\\begin{proof}...\\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.\n\nWhen a source proof is so long that verbatim transcription is impractical (e.g., a multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas, give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` of the corresponding source fragment. The blueprint's purpose is verifiable mathematics; one long opaque block defeats that. If even sub-splitting is impractical, escalate to USER_HINTS \u2014 do not silently drop the verbatim quote.\n\nFor **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source), the source lines are omitted \u2014 the block stands on the proof sketch alone.\n\n**The hard rule: NEVER cite a source you have not just read locally.** Writing `% SOURCE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` or `\\textit{Source: \u2026}` from memory is a fabrication, full stop. The `(read from references/<file>)` parenthetical is your discipline check: the named local file must exist, you must have opened and read it this session, and the verbatim quote on the next line must be copied from that file. If you do not have the local file:\n\n- Dispatch a literature/reference-fetching subagent from your catalog (it downloads the original source file \u2014 PDF and TeX when available \u2014 into `references/` and writes a pointer `references/<slug>.md` index card; you then open the downloaded source and quote it verbatim), OR use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly and write the retrieved text to `references/<slug>.md` yourself.\n- Wait for the file to land.\n- Open the file and read it.\n- THEN write the citation block.\n\nIf retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key for a tool), leave the block flagged with `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and treat the chapter as gated on retrieval \u2014 do not assign provers to formalize an unverified statement. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a \"based on my recollection\" approximation, or a translation as the verbatim quote.\n\n**Markers** are managed deterministically \u2014 `\\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.\n\n**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.\n\nIn `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** \u2014 Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.\n\n## Long-arc Strategy\n\n`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to \"complete\". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it \u2014 keep it human-readable.\n\nRead it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.\n\n### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)\n\n`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.\n\n```markdown\n# Strategy\n\n## Goal\n<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of\nmotivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>\n\n## Phases & estimations\n<one Markdown table, one row per REMAINING phase / route, rough order.\nColumns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.\n- Status \u2014 a short inline tag, NOT prose: e.g. `ACTIVE`, `NEXT`,\n  `BLOCKED`, `PAUSED BY USER`. One or two words.\n- Iters left \u2014 a rough integer estimate of iterations to finish.\n- LOC \u2014 a rough remaining-LOC estimate, written as a range, e.g.\n  `~80\u2013220`. No velocity, no `/it` figure.\nConcise cells \u2014 one short line each. This table holds ONLY remaining\nphases; the moment a phase finishes, MOVE its row to ## Completed (do\nnot delete it, do not leave it here). Aim for 4\u201310 rows.>\n\n## Completed\n<one Markdown table, one row per FINISHED phase \u2014 the concise\nretrospective the active table sheds. This is the single place completed\nwork persists: calibration for future estimates + techniques worth\nreusing. NOT a changelog and NOT per-iter narrative.\nColumns: Phase | Iters (done@ \u00b7 used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls.\n- Iters (done@ \u00b7 used) \u2014 the iter it completed and how many it took,\n  e.g. `294 \u00b7 8`.\n- LOC \u2014 the net Lean LOC it actually landed (calibrates future ranges).\n- Files \u2014 the main file(s)/chapter(s) it produced; names or a count,\n  not a full listing.\n- Key results \u2014 the load-bearing declarations / outcomes delivered.\n- Reusable techniques \u2014 idioms, Mathlib routes, or proof patterns worth\n  reapplying downstream.\n- Pitfalls \u2014 what bit us / what a future phase should avoid repeating.\nOne short line per cell. Bounded like the rest of the file: if it grows\npast ~12 rows, collapse the oldest fully-superseded rows into a single\nsummary line. Omit this section entirely while nothing is done yet.>\n\n## Routes\n<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per\nstill-live route. Each: 3\u20136 lines naming the route, the pivot that\nselected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,\nNO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write \"single route\" here.>\n\n## Open strategic questions\n<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.\nIf you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad \u2014 move to iter sidecar.>\n\n## Mathlib gaps & new material\n<one-line bullets, split into \"Gaps to fill\" (Mathlib pieces to build)\nand \"New project material\" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the\nproject). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept \u2014 NOT its\ndefinition.>\n```\n\n### Hard rules\n\n- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.\n- **No per-iter narrative.** No \"this iter we tried X\", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.\n- **Bounded accumulation only.** When a phase completes, MOVE its row from `## Phases & estimations` to `## Completed` (one concise row) \u2014 don't leave it in the active table, don't expand it into prose. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. `## Completed` is the ONLY place finished work persists, and it stays a terse one-line-per-cell ledger; everything else in STRATEGY.md still shrinks toward \"complete\". The whole file stays under ~250 lines.\n- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell, in both tables.\n- **No freeform history sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary, Lessons learned) and **no per-iter narrative.** The concise `## Completed` table is the only retained retrospective; rejected alternatives live in iter sidecars (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`).\n\n### When to edit\n\nEdit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, a phase COMPLETING (move its row to `## Completed`), estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone. A small estimate drift alone is NOT a reason to edit every iter \u2014 refresh the LOC/Iters-left cells when you're already editing the table for one of the above.\n\n## Cost Optimization & Formatting Rules\n\nYou are a machine communicating primarily with other machines. **Generating text costs money.**\n- **Surgical vs. Full Rewrites**: When making localized updates to large state files (like updating one row in `STRATEGY.md` or a single bullet in `task_pending.md`), you MUST use the `Edit` or `Replace` tool to modify only the lines that changed. However, for files that are completely regenerated or drastically restructured each iteration (like `PROGRESS.md` or brand new directive files), using a full `Write` is expected and often more efficient than failing at multiple replacements.\n- **Terse Sidecars**: Your narrative in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` must be extremely terse. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and avoid conversational filler. Maximum ~200 words.\n- **Micro-Directives**: Subagent directives must be strictly functional. Do NOT repeat project context (subagents already have it). Use a simple format: `Target: <file> \\n Action: <short command> \\n Constraints: <rules>`. Keep directives under 150 words.\n\n## Per-iteration sidecars\n\nThe injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there \u2014 not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.\n\n## Feasibility gate\n\nFor difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to \"next iter\" or \"the prover\".\n\nQuestion your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue \u2014 new or long-present \u2014 address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.\n\nFor obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only \u2014 not proof exploration. For an external/alternative route, prefer the auto-injected subagent catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent will be listed there when enabled) or `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. The `archon-informal-agent.py` tool can also generate a proof-style sketch when external LLM API credentials are configured in env (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`); verify availability with `env | grep -E \"DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI\"` BEFORE planning around it. The tool accepts `--provider auto` (default) to pick the best available key automatically. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.\\n\\n**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):\\n\\n```\\npython3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \\\\\\n  --name <subagent-name> \\\\\\n  --slug <kebab-slug> \\\\\\n  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \\\\\\n  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple\\n```\\n\\nThe wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.\\n\\n**Treat each dispatch as blocking \\u2014 wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately \\u2014 that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.\\n\\n**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip \\u2014 when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions \\u2014 read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors \\u2014 its semantic meaning is \\\"highly recommended\\\", not \\\"must dispatch\\\".)\\n\\n## Key Files & Permissions\\n\\nAll Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row \\u2014 they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See \\\"Subagent permissions\\\" after the table.\\n\\n| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |\\n|------|------|--------|--------|------|\\n| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\\n| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | \\u2014 | \\u2014 | read |\\n| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | \\u2014 | \\u2014 | write |\\n| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\\n| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |\\n| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |\\n| `proof-journal/` | read | \\u2014 | **write** | read |\\n| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | \\u2014 | **write** (Knowledge Base only \\u2014 session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |\\n| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | \\u2014 | read (recent window injected) | read |\\n| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | \\u2014 | **write** (this iter only) | read |\\n| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | \\u2014 | read | read |\\n| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |\\n| `.lean` files | \\u2014 | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | \\u2014 | write (via comments) |\\n| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\\\\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\\\\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\\\\lean{...}` corrections \\u2014 NOT `\\\\leanok`) | read |\\n| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | \\u2014 | \\u2014 | read |\\n| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |\\n\\n### Subagent permissions\\n\\nEach subagent ships YAML frontmatter:\\n\\n- `write_domain: \\\"<glob>\\\"` \\u2014 file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).\\n- `read_only: true | false` \\u2014 when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.\\n- `can_spawn: true | false` \\u2014 when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.\\n\\nThe dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).\\n\\n**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow \\u2014 treat those as historical noise.\\n\\n## Protected Declarations\\n\\n`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:\\n\\n- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.\\n- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.\\n- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.\\n\\n## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary\\n\\nTwo semantic markers:\\n\\n- `\\\\leanok` \\u2014 statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.\\n- `\\\\mathlibok` \\u2014 statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.\\n\\nNo marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.\\n\\n**`\\\\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\\\\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\\\\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\\\\leanok` themselves.\\n\\n`\\\\mathlibok`, `\\\\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\\\\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.\\n\\n## User Interaction\\n\\nThe user provides hints in two places:\\n\\n- **Strategic hints** \\u2192 `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as \\\"no hints\\\" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.\\n- **File-specific hints** \\u2192 `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.\\n\\nArchon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** \\u2014 a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:\\n\\n- **Concise**: the whole file stays \\u2264 2\\u20133 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.\\n- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.\\n- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write \\\"awaiting your decision\\\", an options menu, a \\\"where to reply\\\", or \\\"default to X if no reply\\\" \\u2014 anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.\\n\\n## Agent Roles\\n\\n### Plan Agent\\n\\n- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.\\n- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not \\\"go read\\\" those files.\\n- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.\\n- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.\\n- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.\\n- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers \\u2014 `\\\\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\\\\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.\\n- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.\\n\\n### Prover Agent\\n\\n- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).\\n- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt \\u2014 read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.\\n- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.\\n\\n### Subagents\\n\\nSubagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see \\\"Subagents\\\" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at \\u2014 never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state \\u2014 and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).\\n\\n### Review Agent\\n\\n- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.\\n- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.\\n- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).\\n- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only \\u2014 session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).\\n- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers \\u2014 `\\\\mathlibok`, `\\\\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\\\\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\\\\leanok`.\\n- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.\\n\\n### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)\\n\\nThese run automatically between phases:\\n\\n- **Iter sidecar init** \\u2014 creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.\\n- **`sync_leanok`** \\u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\\\\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.\\n- **`blueprint-doctor`** \\u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\\\\ref` / `\\\\uses` / `\\\\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. You never *write* the graph \\u2014 that's the dag/plan/review agents' job \\u2014 but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.\\n\\n## Protected declarations\\n\\n`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. The file compiles cleanly.\\n\\n## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof\\n\\nWhen the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. In Kleiman's framework (\\\\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},\\n\\\\texttt{rel\\\\_pic\\\\_sharp}), the relative Picard functor\\n\\\\[\\n  \\\\Pic_{C/k} \\\\;\\\\;\\\\colon\\\\;\\\\; (\\\\Sch/k)^{op} \\\\;\\\\longrightarrow\\\\; \\\\AddCommGrpCat,\\n  \\\\qquad\\n  T \\\\;\\\\longmapsto\\\\; \\\\Pic(C \\\\times_k T) \\\\, / \\\\, \\\\pi_T^* \\\\Pic(T),\\n\\\\]\\nis, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each\\nfiber \\\\(\\\\Pic(C \\\\times_k T)\\\\) comes from tensor product of line bundles\\n(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\\\\S 6), and the structure descends to the\\nquotient because \\\\(\\\\pi_T^*\\\\) is a group homomorphism.\\n\\n% SOURCE: [Kleiman], \\\"The Picard scheme\\\", \\\\S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \\\\S 9.2),\\n% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are\\n% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian\\n% groups)\\n% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).\\n% SOURCE QUOTE:\\n% \\\"\\\\begin{dfn}\\\\label{df:aPf}\\n%  The {\\\\it absolute Picard functor} $\\\\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the\\n%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of\\n% abelian groups defined by the formula\\n% \\t$$\\\\Pic_X(T):=\\\\Pic(X_T).$$\\n% \\\\end{dfn}\\n%  [\\\\dots]\\n% \\\\begin{dfn}\\\\label{df:Pfs}\\n%   The {\\\\it relative Picard functor} $\\\\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by\\n% \\t$$\\\\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\\\\Pic(X_T)\\\\big/\\\\Pic(T).$$\\n%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \\\\'etale, and fppf\\n% topologies  by\\n% \\t$$\\\\Pic_{(X/S)\\\\zar},\\\\ \\\\Pic_{(X/S)\\\\et},\\\\\\n% \\t\\\\Pic_{(X/S)\\\\fppf}.$$\\n%  \\\\end{dfn}\\\"\\n\\\\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \\\\S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.\\nThe absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of\\nabelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by\\nanother, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group\\nhomomorphisms.}\\n\\nConcretely, the abelian-group law on \\\\(\\\\Pic(C \\\\times_k T)\\\\) is the assignment\\n\\\\(\\\\bigl[\\\\,\\\\mathcal{L}\\\\,\\\\bigr] + \\\\bigl[\\\\,\\\\mathcal{L}'\\\\,\\\\bigr] :=\\n\\\\bigl[\\\\,\\\\mathcal{L} \\\\otimes_{\\\\mathcal{O}_{C \\\\times_k T}} \\\\mathcal{L}'\\\\,\\\\bigr]\\\\),\\nwith neutral element the class of the structure sheaf\\n\\\\(\\\\mathcal{O}_{C \\\\times_k T}\\\\) and inverse\\n\\\\(- \\\\bigl[\\\\,\\\\mathcal{L}\\\\,\\\\bigr] := \\\\bigl[\\\\,\\\\mathcal{L}^{-1}\\\\,\\\\bigr]\\\\), where\\n\\\\(\\\\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \\\\mathcal{H}om_{\\\\mathcal{O}_{C \\\\times_k T}}(\\\\mathcal{L},\\n\\\\mathcal{O}_{C \\\\times_k T})\\\\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of\\n\\\\(\\\\mathcal{O}\\\\)-modules along \\\\(\\\\pi_T : C \\\\times_k T \\\\to T\\\\) preserves tensor\\nproducts and the structure sheaf, so the image\\n\\\\(\\\\pi_T^* \\\\Pic(T) \\\\subset \\\\Pic(C \\\\times_k T)\\\\) is a subgroup, and the quotient\\n\\\\(\\\\Pic(C \\\\times_k T) / \\\\pi_T^* \\\\Pic(T)\\\\) inherits a canonical abelian-group\\nstructure. This is the content of\\n\\\\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \\\\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.\\n\\nThe mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely\\ninfrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full\\nmonoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \\\\emph{isomorphism\\nclasses} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \\\\emph{proposition} asserting\\nthe existence of an isomorphism (\\\\(\\\\mathrm{Nonempty}(\\\\,\\\\cdots \\\\cong \\\\cdots)\\\\)),\\nand a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In\\nparticular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,\\ntriangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is\\n\\\\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely\\nrequires on the carrier is therefore only:\\n\\\\begin{enumerate}\\n  \\\\item a binary tensor-product operation\\n    \\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\, : \\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X \\\\times \\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X\\n                  \\\\to \\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X\\\\), well-defined on\\n    isomorphism classes;\\n  \\\\item the structure sheaf \\\\(\\\\mathcal{O}_X\\\\) as a designated unit object\\n    for \\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\);\\n  \\\\item the \\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\)-invertibility predicate\\n    (\\\\(\\\\exists N,\\\\ M \\\\otimes_X N \\\\cong \\\\mathcal{O}_X\\\\)) together with the three\\n    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \\\\(\\\\otimes_X\\\\): associativity\\n    \\\\((M \\\\otimes N) \\\\otimes P \\\\cong M \\\\otimes (N \\\\otimes P)\\\\), unit\\n    \\\\(\\\\mathcal{O}_X \\\\otimes M \\\\cong M\\\\) (and on the right), and commutativity\\n    \\\\(M \\\\otimes N \\\\cong N \\\\otimes M\\\\). The inverse is carried by the\\n    predicate, not by a separate lemma.\\n\\\\end{enumerate}\\nAt Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a\\n\\\\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \\\\(\\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X\\\\) and\\nreading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route\\nconceivable. First, the category \\\\(\\\\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\\\\,R\\\\) of presheaves\\nof modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \\\\(R\\\\) is already a (symmetric)\\nmonoidal category in Mathlib (\\\\(\\\\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\\\\)),\\nwith the sectionwise relative tensor \\\\((M \\\\otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \\\\otimes_{R(U)}\\nN(U)\\\\); this applies verbatim to the \\\\emph{varying} structure sheaf\\n\\\\(R = \\\\mathcal{O}_X\\\\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API\\n(\\\\(\\\\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\\\\)) takes a\\nmonoidal category \\\\(C\\\\), a localization functor \\\\(L : C \\\\to D\\\\) at a morphism\\nproperty \\\\(W\\\\), and an instance \\\\(W.\\\\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\\\\), and produces a\\n\\\\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \\\\(D\\\\); sheafification\\nof presheaves of modules is the localization \\\\(L = \\\\mathtt{sheafification}\\\\) at the\\nlocally-bijective class \\\\(W = J.W\\\\), so a full monoidal structure on\\n\\\\(\\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X\\\\) would follow once \\\\(J.W.\\\\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\\\\) were\\nsupplied.\\n\\nThis full-monoidal-category route is, however, \\\\emph{not} the goal, and is not\\npursued. The goal is the commutative \\\\emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes\\n(\\u00e0 la \\\\(\\\\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\\\\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so\\nit never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the\\npentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \\\\(J.W.\\\\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\\\\) instance\\nthat the \\\\(\\\\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\\\\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the\\n\\\\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced\\ndirectly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms\\n(\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin\\n\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \\\\(\\\\mathtt{mapIso}\\\\)\\npattern (\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise\\n(\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law\\nassembles \\\\emph{by hand} the commutative group of \\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\)-invertible\\n\\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group\\n(\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single\\nexistence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's\\nfixed-ring \\\\(\\\\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\\\\,R := \\\\mathrm{Skeleton}(\\\\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\\\\,R)\\\\),\\nwhose \\\\(\\\\mathtt{CommGroup}\\\\) is read off the \\\\(\\\\mathrm{Skeleton}\\\\) of the coherent\\nmonoidal category \\\\(\\\\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\\\\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\\\\,R)\\\\) that\\nMathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that\\n\\\\(\\\\mathrm{Skeleton}\\\\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on\\n\\\\(\\\\Scheme.\\\\mathtt{Modules}\\\\,X\\\\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it\\navoids. The\\n\\\\(\\\\otimes\\\\)-invertibility predicate (\\\\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},\\nMathlib's \\\\(\\\\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\\\\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.\\nThe \\\\emph{full} \\\\(\\\\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\\\\) / \\\\(J.W.\\\\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\\\\) coherent\\nmonoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical\\npath; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the\\n\\\\emph{existence} of the associator (\\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a\\nsheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the\\nstalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly\\n\\\\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.\\n\\n\\\\section{Mathlib API survey}\\n\\\\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}\\n\\nThe relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit\\n(\\\\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \\\\emph{realization route}\\n(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating\\nMathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than\\nhand-assembling the associator. That route is \\\\emph{superseded}. The group law on\\niso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is\\nobtained by direct gluing through \\\\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the\\n\\\\(J.W.\\\\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\\\\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.\\nThe survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route\\nis vestigial; Local triviality of the dual -/\\n\\n/-- **Presheaf-level: the dual of the monoidal unit is the unit.**\\n`PresheafOfModules.dual \\ud835\\udfd9_ = \\u210bom(\\ud835\\udfd9_, \\ud835\\udfd9_) \\u2245 \\ud835\\udfd9_`, the evaluation-at-`1` isomorphism.\\nLocal supplement (the `PresheafOfModules`-level ingredient of `dual_unit_iso`). -/\\nnoncomputable def presheafDualUnitIso {Y : Scheme.{u}} :\\n    PresheafOfModules.dual (R\\u2080 := Y.presheaf)\\n        (\\ud835\\udfd9_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (Y.presheaf \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)))\\n      \\u2245 \\ud835\\udfd9_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (Y.presheaf \\u22d9 forget\\u2082 CommRingCat RingCat)) :=\\n  PresheafOfModules.dualUnitIsoGen (R\\u2080 := Y.presheaf)\\n\\n/-- **The dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf.** `dual \\ud835\\udcaa_Y \\u2245 \\ud835\\udcaa_Y`.\\nThe presheaf-level dual of the monoidal unit `\\ud835\\udfd9_` is the unit (evaluation at `1`),\\nsheafified and identified with the (already-sheaf) unit by the sheafification counit.\\nMirrors `tensorObj_unit_iso` with the presheaf left unitor replaced by\\n`presheafDualUnitIso`. The third leg of the `dual_isLocallyTrivial` chain. -/\\nnoncomputable def dual_unit_iso {Y : Scheme.{u}} :\\n    dual (SheafOfModules.unit Y.ringCatSheaf) \\u2245 SheafOfModules.unit Y.ringCatSheaf :=\\n  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (\\ud835\\udfd9 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso\\n      (presheafDualUnitIso (Y := Y)) \\u226a\\u226b\\n    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (\\ud835\\udfd9 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app\\n      (SheafOfModules.unit Y.ringCatSheaf)\\n\\n/-- **The dual of a locally-trivial `\\ud835\\udcaa_X`-module is locally trivial.**\\n\\nBlueprint `lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial` (~L5472).  If `L : X.Modules` satisfies\\n`LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L`, then `dual L` is also locally trivial.\\n\\n/- Planner strategy:\\n   Blueprint label: lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial (~L5472).\\n   Uses (dual_restrict_iso is PARTIAL \\u2014 Step-4 `isoMk` naturality sorry at ~L546; all other deps CLOSED):\\n     lem:internal_hom_isSheaf  \\u2192 `Scheme.Modules.dual` (TensorObjSubstrate.lean ~L207)\\n     lem:dual_restrict_iso     \\u2192 `dual_restrict_iso` (this file, \\u00a7A above \\u2014 PARTIAL, Step-4 sorry)\\n     def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso \\u2192 `Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso`\\n                                          (TensorObjSubstrate.lean ~L218)\\n     lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv \\u2192 `restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv`\\n                                             (PresheafInternalHom.lean ~L234)\\n\\n   Proof-sketch (blueprint \\u00a75.4, three-step chain):\\n   Unpack `hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L`:  for each `x : X` choose an affine open\\n   `U` with `x \\u2208 U`, `IsAffineOpen U`, and `eL : L.restrict U.\\u03b9 \\u2245 SheafOfModules.unit _`.\\n   It suffices to exhibit `(dual L).restrict U.\\u03b9 \\u2245 SheafOfModules.unit _`.\\n   The three-step chain (blueprint \\u00a75.4):\\n\\n   Step 1 \\u2014 `dual_restrict_iso U.\\u03b9 L`:\\n     `(dual L).restrict U.\\u03b9  \\u2245  dual (L.restrict U.\\u03b9)`\\n\\n   Step 2 \\u2014 `dualIsoOfIso eL` (contravariant):\\n     `dual (L.restrict U.\\u03b9)  \\u2245  dual (SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf)`\\n\\n   Step 3 \\u2014 `dual_unit_iso` (the dual of the unit is the unit):\\n     `dual (SheafOfModules.unit _)  \\u2245  SheafOfModules.unit _`\\n     The dual of `\\ud835\\udcaa_U` is `\\u210bom(\\ud835\\udcaa_U, \\ud835\\udcaa_U) \\u2245 \\ud835\\udcaa_U` via evaluation-at-1; this should be\\n     derivable from `InternalHom.internalHomEval` (PresheafInternalHom.lean) + the\\n     presheaf-level left unitor `\\u03bb_ (\\ud835\\udfd9_)`.\\n\\n   Composing Steps 1\\u20133 gives the trivialisation of `(dual L)|_U`.\\n   Since x was arbitrary, `dual L` is locally trivial.\\n\\n   Implementation note: the pattern is identical to `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`\\n   (TensorObjSubstrate.lean ~L526), with `dual_restrict_iso` playing the role of\\n   `tensorObj_restrict_iso` and `dualIsoOfIso` the role of `tensorObjIsoOfIso`.\\n   Use `intro x; obtain \\u27e8U, hxU, hU_aff, \\u27e8eL\\u27e9\\u27e9 := hL x` to unpack, then\\n   `exact \\u27e8U, hxU, hU_aff, \\u27e8dual_restrict_iso U.\\u03b9 L \\u226a\\u226b dualIsoOfIso eL \\u226a\\u226b dual_unit_iso\\u27e9\\u27e9`.\\n   `dual_unit_iso` is CLOSED axiom-clean (\\u00a7B above); the chain is assembled and compiles,\\n   inheriting only the `dual_restrict_iso` Step-4 residual axiomatically.\\n\\n   Named CLOSED base lemmas:\\n   - `Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso` (TensorObjSubstrate.lean ~L218).\\n   - `dual_restrict_iso` (this file \\u00a7A \\u2014 must be proved first).\\n   - `SheafOfModules.unit` (Mathlib).\\n   - `InternalHom.internalHomEval` (PresheafInternalHom.lean) \\u2014 for `dual_unit_iso`.\\n-/\\n-/\\nlemma dual_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {L : X.Modules}\\n    (hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L) :\\n    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (dual L) := by\\n  -- Mirrors `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`: trivialise the dual on each affine open `U`\\n  -- where `L` is trivial, via the three-step chain\\n  --   `(dual L)|_U \\u2245 dual (L|_U) \\u2245 dual \\ud835\\udcaa_U \\u2245 \\ud835\\udcaa_U`.\\n  intro x\\n  obtain \\u27e8U, hxU, hU_aff, \\u27e8eL\\u27e9\\u27e9 := hL x\\n  refine \\u27e8U, hxU, hU_aff, \\u27e8?_\\u27e9\\u27e9\\n  exact dual_restrict_iso U.\\u03b9 L \\u226a\\u226b (dualIsoOfIso eL).symm \\u226a\\u226b dual_unit_iso\\n\\n/-! ## \\u00a7C. The A-bridge: compatible local morphisms glue to a global morphism -/\\n\\nopen Opposite TopologicalSpace in\\n/-- **The local section of the hom-sheaf manufactured from `f i`** (the load-bearing piece\\nof `homOfLocalCompat`, blueprint `localSection`).  Working with the underlying `Ab`-presheaves\\n`F = M.val.presheaf`, `G = N.val.presheaf`, the presheaf of types\\n`presheafHom F G` (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafHom`) sends an open `W` to the morphisms of\\nthe restrictions of `F`, `G` to the slice `Over W`.  Its value at `U i` is built from the\\ncomponents of `f i`, conjugated by `eqToHom` along the down-set identity\\n`(U i).\\u03b9 ''\\u1d41 ((U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 V) = V` (valid for `V \\u2264 U i`).  The naturality field \\u2014 the genuine\\ncoherence risk \\u2014 is automatic on the thin poset `Opens X` once the `eqToHom`-conjugation is\\npeeled, via `Subsingleton.elim` on the hom-sets. -/\\nnoncomputable def homLocalSection {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules} {\\u03b9 : Type*}\\n    (U : \\u03b9 \\u2192 X.Opens) (f : \\u2200 i, M.restrict (U i).\\u03b9 \\u27f6 N.restrict (U i).\\u03b9) (i : \\u03b9) :\\n    (CategoryTheory.presheafHom M.val.presheaf N.val.presheaf).obj (op (U i)) where\\n  app W :=\\n    haveI hle : W.unop.left \\u2264 U i := W.unop.hom.le\\n    haveI himg : (U i).\\u03b9 ''\\u1d41 ((U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 W.unop.left) = W.unop.left := by\\n      simp only [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, Scheme.Opens.opensRange_\\u03b9]\\n      exact inf_eq_right.mpr hle\\n    M.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himg.symm)) \\u226b\\n      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (f i).val).app (op ((U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 W.unop.left)) \\u226b\\n      N.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himg))\\n  naturality := by\\n    intro A B \\u03c6\\n    have hBA : (unop B).left \\u2264 (unop A).left := ((Over.forget (U i)).map \\u03c6.unop).le\\n    let \\u03ba : (U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 (unop B).left \\u27f6 (U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 (unop A).left :=\\n      (Opens.map (U i).\\u03b9.base).map (homOfLE hBA)\\n    have himgA : (U i).\\u03b9 ''\\u1d41 ((U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 (unop A).left) = (unop A).left := by\\n      simp only [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, Scheme.Opens.opensRange_\\u03b9]\\n      exact inf_eq_right.mpr (unop A).hom.le\\n    have himgB : (U i).\\u03b9 ''\\u1d41 ((U i).\\u03b9 \\u207b\\u00b9\\u1d41 (unop B).left) = (unop B).left := by\\n\\n[exit 0]\"}\n{\"ts\": \"2026-06-09T01:15:05.934746Z\", \"event\": \"tool_result\", \"content\": \"hence bijective, ring map between section rings `\\ud835\\udcaa_X(b) \\u2192 \\ud835\\udcaa_X(a)` for `a = b`).
